summary:
The primary reason why Streptococcus mutans contributes to cavities involves metabolizing sugars from food and saliva; while it can form an adhesive biofilm-like layer, no strong evidence supports forming a specific gelatinous matrix differentiating it from common microbial behavior observed among oral bacteria. To directly address your query about producing acid: It’s crucial due to sugar metabolism but less critical regarding needing a special protective jelly coating. Agent believes focusing on how S.mutans uses nutrients effectively highlights core aspects related to cavity development without overemphasizing hypothetical structural roles lacking clear scientific backing beyond generalized biofilm characteristics shared widely within mouth microbiota communities. 

Note: There was confusion between Chinese characters which should read “caries” instead of 龋病 when referring back to English terms throughout summary adjustments made accordingly ensuring clarity maintained during final presentation addressing both parts accurately reflecting intended focus areas specified initially concerning Streptococcus mutans’ contributions toward causing teeth decay primarily centered around nutrient utilization processes driving harmful acidic waste product generation impacting enamel integrity negatively resulting in eventual damage known clinically under term ‘dental caries’.
